coredata.dc:title: Transfection and intracellular trafficking properties of carbon dot-gold nanoparticle molecular assembly conjugated with PEI-pDNA
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Jinhwan
Park, Juhee
Kim, Hyunwoo
Singha, Kaushik
Kim, Won Jong
coredata.dc:description: The work employs carbon dot (CD) which has been emerging as a fluorescent nanomaterial with excellent biocompatibility and perceived as a promising alternative to quantum dot (QD), to monitor the association/dissociation of polymeric carrier/plasmid DNA (pDNA) complex during transfection. To shed light on the underlying post-endosomal events and provide the insight to design rational and efficient gene delivery vector, the adopted strategy exploited the quenching of the fluorescence of CD by Au nanoparticles. The surface of CD and Au was modified with highly cationic polymer, polyethylenimine (PEI) and subsequent treatment with non-labeled pDNA gave rise to quenched delivery complex. High salt concentration triggered the dissociation of the complex with accompanied fluorescence recovery arising due to the increase in distance between CD and Au. The studies revealed the potential of the developed CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA ternary nano-assembly as a highly efficient hybrid transfecting agent with high cell viability under the optimum condition. The changes occurred at the intracellular level during transfection especially post-endosomal step were monitored by fluorescence measurement using fluorescence microscope. This nano-assembly system was found to be very effective at monitoring the carrier/pDNA dissociation in a non-labeled manner, thus provides efficient strategy to study the mechanistic aspect of polymer-mediated pDNA delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Gold nanoparticle
Polyethylenimine
Gene delivery
Molecular assembly
Real-time monitoring

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Biomaterials for the Delivery of Drugs, Genes, Vaccines and Active Molecules

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Association and dissociation of CD-PEI/Au-PEI assembly modulated by pDNA
Gene transfection efficiency and cell viability mediated by carriers
Cellular trafficking of association/dissociation of molecular assembly

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration for the gene delivery and real-time monitoring of cellular trafficking utilizing CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA molecular assembly of nanohybrids.

Synthesis and characterization of CD-PEI. (a) Synthetic scheme of CD-PEI. (b) HAADF TEM image of CD-PEI. The bright region represents CD core. (c) The fluorescence spectra of CD-PEI. Inset spectra represent normalized fluorescence of CD-PEI. (d) CD-PEI fluorescence under UV illumination. (e) FT-IR spectra of bare PEI and CD-PEI. (f) Zeta potential of bare CD and CD-PEI.

Synthesis and characterization of Au-PEI. (a) Synthetic scheme of Au nanoparticle. (b) Surface functionalization strategy of Au-PEI. (c) Top; Au surface plasmon profile depending on the concentration of lipoic acid-PEI. Concentration of lipoic acid-PEI; control (0 M), 200 nM, 2 μM, 200 μM, 400 μM, 800 μM, 1.2 mM, 1.6 mM, 2 mM. Bottom; aggregation formation in the mixture of Au and bare PEI. Concentration of PEI; 400 μM, 800 μM, 1.2 mM, 1.6 mM, 2 mM (d) TEM image of Au-PEI. (e) The absorbance spectra of Au-PEI and Au-cit. (f) Zeta potential of Au-cit and Au-PEI.

Quenching profile of AuNP. (a) Schematic illustrations of dynamic quenching and static quenching. (b) Relative fluorescence intensity of CD-PEI at 465 nm after mixing with Au-PEI (black square) and Au-lipo (red circle). (c) Image of CD-PEI under UV illumination (top) and visible light (bottom) when mixed with different amount of Au-lipo (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

pDNA-mediated fluorescence quenching and recovery profile. (a) Fluorescence spectra were obtained by adding pDNA into CD-PEI/Au-PEI mixture. The inset represents relative fluorescence intensity at 465 nm (b) TEM image of CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complex. (c) Salt concentration dependent fluorescence displacement. The inset represents relative fluorescence at 465 nm at different NaCl concentration. (d) UV illumination of CD-PEI/Au-PEI mixture (left), CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complex (middle), and CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complex with salt (right).

Fluorescence spectra obtained by complexation with pDNA and dissociation with NaCl. Fluorescence spectra after adding salt into CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA molecular assembly (a) and into CD-PEI-Au-PEI chemical conjugate with pDNA (b).

Gel retardation of pDNA with different carrier system. (a) CD-PEI/pDNA, (b) Au-PEI/pDNA, (c) CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA, (d) Bare CD/pDNA, (e) Au-cit/pDNA. Lane 1; pDNA only, lane 2; N/P = 0.5, lane 3; N/P = 1, lane 4; N/P = 2, lane 5; N/P = 10.
Transfection efficiency study using luciferase reporter gene assay in (a) HeLa and (b) PC-3 cells at N/P = 20. Cell viability using MTT assay in (c) HeLa and (d) PC-3 cells at N/P = 20.

Cellular imaging of CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA assembly in HeLa cells. (a) Fluorescence of CD-PEI (blue), (b) TOTO-iodide labeled pDNA (red), and (c) merged image at different time interval. Scale bar is 20 μm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(a) Cellular imaging of CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complex in HeLa cells. Fluorescence of CD-PEI (blue) in chemically conjugated CD-PEI-Au-PEI system (top) and without Au-PEI system (bottom) at different time interval. (b) Fluorescence signal intensity graph. Relative signal intensity is defined as the total integration of blue signal over cell number (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

head.ce:title: Transfection and intracellular trafficking properties of carbon dot-gold nanoparticle molecular assembly conjugated with PEI-pDNA

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Self-assembly and Complexity, Institute for Basic Science, and Department of Chemistry, Polymer Research Institute, Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), Pohang 790-784, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Basic Science, and Department of Chemistry
Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The work employs carbon dot (CD) which has been emerging as a fluorescent nanomaterial with excellent biocompatibility and perceived as a promising alternative to quantum dot (QD), to monitor the association/dissociation of polymeric carrier/plasmid DNA (pDNA) complex during transfection. To shed light on the underlying post-endosomal events and provide the insight to design rational and efficient gene delivery vector, the adopted strategy exploited the quenching of the fluorescence of CD by Au nanoparticles. The surface of CD and Au was modified with highly cationic polymer, polyethylenimine (PEI) and subsequent treatment with non-labeled pDNA gave rise to quenched delivery complex. High salt concentration triggered the dissociation of the complex with accompanied fluorescence recovery arising due to the increase in distance between CD and Au. The studies revealed the potential of the developed CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA ternary nano-assembly as a highly efficient hybrid transfecting agent with high cell viability under the optimum condition. The changes occurred at the intracellular level during transfection especially post-endosomal step were monitored by fluorescence measurement using fluorescence microscope. This nano-assembly system was found to be very effective at monitoring the carrier/pDNA dissociation in a non-labeled manner, thus provides efficient strategy to study the mechanistic aspect of polymer-mediated pDNA delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: The impetus to develop polymeric vectors for gene delivery has been drifted from viral vectors mainly due to the safety and less viable production concerns associated with viral vectors [1–5]. However, polymeric vectors are impaired significantly with low delivery efficiency compared to viral vectors [6–8], and thus to overcome diverse impediments plethora of research activities have been undertaken employing various strategies and hybrid integrated systems, to mention a few, polymer-inorganic particle hybrid system, crosslinking of polymer system, and so on [9–14]. Nevertheless, for practical clinical realization it is essential to find out rational design method for polymeric gene delivery system with high efficiency. To develop biologically viable and efficient delivery constructs, it is of paramount importance to decipher the underlying mechanisms of polymer-mediated delivery which comprises several extra- or intracellular events such as circulation in blood stream, cellular uptake, endosomal escape, and dissociation of cationic polymer/gene complex (polyplex) [15–17]. However, post-endosomal escape the dissociation of polyplex is considered as the rate-limiting step for efficient trafficking of gene to nucleus. A prompt and opportune dissociation of polyplex bears an immense importance in achieving high gene transfection efficiency, however, only a few work has highlighted this aspect [18,19]. Therefore, there is a pressing need to develop tools which can monitor the dissociation of polyplex in real-time without compromising the delivery efficiency and the safety issues.

In practice, organic dyes are employed to label polymeric vectors and plasmid DNA (pDNA) to study polymer/pDNA dissociation and trafficking [20], however, organic dyes generally show photobleaching and instability which render them inappropriate for monitoring of polyplex dissociation under biological setup [21]. As an alternative quantum dot (QD) which possesses excellent optical properties, such as good photostability, less photobleaching, narrow fluorescence spectra, and size-tunable emission has been used for labeling the materials [22–24]. Especially, its use in combination with organic dyes to obtain fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) in constructing vector and pDNA complexes with distance dependent optical properties is worth mentioning and provides the provision to monitor association and dissociation of polyplexes [25–27]. However, such systems raise a detrimental biocompatibility issues due to the toxic nature of heavy metal which can be accumulated in the body and thereby elicit potential long term toxicity [28–31]. Furthermore, such systems require complicated modifications of nucleotide and dye-labeled pDNA and are known to impede intracellular transcription to mRNA; hence this approach is hard to apply in image-guided gene delivery system.

Recently, quantum sized carbon-dot (CD), which is composed of carbon and oxygen, and being passivated by polymer has been reported and the fluorescent nanomaterial shows excellent optical properties like high quantum yield and excitation wavelength dependent multi-color emission as well as environmental and biological compatibility [32–34]. Furthermore, its chemical inertness, good colloidal stability, and easy preparation promote CD as an appropriate alternative to QD. Above all, heavy metal-free CD shows low cytotoxicity which is one of the most important requisite for biological applications. Many strategies for synthesizing CD have been developed such as laser ablation, thermal carbonization, electrochemical preparation, and microwave-assisted pyrolysis [32,33,35,36]. In particular, microwave-assisted pyrolysis has been widely used due to its easy, fast, and environmental friendly green synthesis [36–40]. All these favorable optical, chemical properties and the facile synthesis render CD an enticing and promising candidate for bioimaging exploitations.

The aim of this study is to demonstrate the capability of enhanced gene delivery and real-time monitoring of intra-cellular movement and fate of the carriers and concomitant gene transfection utilizing polyethylenimine (PEI)-functionalized CD (CD-PEI) and PEI-functionalized gold nanoparticle (Au-PEI) nanohybrids. Herein, we develop a delivery system to monitor carrier/pDNA dissociation with intracellular gene delivery by modulating molecular assembly of nanohybrids.

Cationic fluorophore and cationic quencher should form a complex with negative charged pDNA through an electrostatic interaction, leading to the fluorescence quenching. Any suppression or lessening of the attractive electrostatic force is expected to facilitate the dissociation of the complex by allowing the cationic components to part with the negatively charged pDNA, and consequently the distance between fluorophore and quencher is increased more than the Foster distance due to charge repulsion, inducing the fluorescence recovery. Excellent optical property and biocompatibility make CD an enticing material and has been employed as a fluorophore after being surface functionalized with PEI, a prevalently used cationic polymer for gene delivery. Au-PEI is used as a quencher to investigate molecular assembly. We envisioned that pDNA induced complex formation between negatively charged pDNA and the CD-PEI and Au-PEI carriers, should trigger the quenching of the fluorescence arising from CD-PEI due to reduced distance between CD-PEI and Au-PEI. It is expected that upon internalization of the complex, pDNA should dissociate from the complex which is essential not only for initiating transfection but also for eliciting recovery of fluorescence from CD-PEI. Therefore, a simple measurement of the fluorescence changes at the intracellular level could enable us to monitor the association and dissociation of carrier/pDNA easily in real-time without any labeling of pDNA and further facilitate to achieve efficient gene delivery (Fig. 1 ).

As there is no effort that has been directed to utilize such molecular assembly system comprising Au-PEI and CD-PEI to accomplish gene transfection we consider this approach could provide significant insight onto its potential as delivery agent with unique ability to assist in monitoring of pDNA release after internalization.

Glycerol (ultrapure, molecular biology grade) was purchased from Affymetrix (Cleveland, USA). Polyethylenimine (PEI, Mw = 25 kDa and 1.2 kDa) was obtained from Polysciences, Inc. (Warrington, UK). Gold chloride hydrate (HAuCl4), sodium citrate tribasic dehydrate (NaCit), α-lipoic acid, potassium bromide (KBr), 1-ethyl-3-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), sodium chloride (NaCl), 4-(2-hydroxy-ethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) buffer solution, and suberic acid bis(N-hydroxysuccinimide ester) (DSS) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was obtained from Fluka (China). Acetonitrile (ACN, HPLC ultra gradient solvent) was received from J.T.Barker (USA). All commercial reagents were used without further purification. Dialysis membrane (MWCO: 25 kDa) was purchased from Spectrum Laboratories (Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA). TOTO-3 iodide was purchased from Invitrogen, Inc. (Eugene, Oregon, USA), and mounting medium for fluorescence was purchased from VECTOR (Burlingame, USA).

CD-PEI was synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrolysis of glycerol [40]. Glycerol (22 g) was mixed with PEI (0.5 g, MW = 25 kDa) and phosphate buffer (6 mL, 10 mM, pH = 7.4) and stirred vigorously overnight. Then, it was heated using commercial microwave oven (700 W) for 10 min, allowed to cool down. The sample was diluted with distilled water and dialyzed using cellulose membrane dialysis bag (MWCO = 25 kDa) against pure water for 3 days. The collected yellow product was lyophilized. The final product was dissolved in D.I. water and stored at 4 °C without any further purification.

Au nanoparticle was synthesized by reduction of HAuCl4 at high temperature in the presence of citrate ion which is mild reductant and stabilizer [41]. 100 mL of HAuCl4 solution (1.47 mM in D.W.) was prepared at 120 °C. After 20 min, 2 mL of citrate solution (0.34 M in D.W.) was added into the HAuCl4 solution and the color of solution changes to dark red. This solution was cooled down in the room temperature and stored at 4 °C without any further purification.

The lipoic acid conjugated PEI was synthesized from lipoic acid precursor. First, the NHS ester is conjugated at lipoic acid (NHS-lipo) using EDC/NHS chemistry in DMF condition for 4 h at room temperature. A solution of lipoic acid (0.540 g), N-hydroxysuccinimide (0.330 g), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide hydrochloride (0.550 g) in 10 mL of anhydrous DMF was stirred at room temperature for 4 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The solution was diluted with 50 mL EtOAc and washed with H2O. The organic layer was dried over MgSO4, filtered off, and concentrated in vacuo. Purification by recrystalization (EtOAc/hexane) afforded NHS-lipo as a light yellow crystal. Then the PEI (MW = 1.2 k) was conjugated by simply mixing PEI in HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) and NHS-lipo in acetonitrile (ACN).

Lipoic acid-PEI conjugate was simply mixed with Au nanoparticle. To ensure complete citrate-thiol exchange, 250 μL of Au nanoparticle (8 nM) was added into the different concentration of lipoic acid-PEI solution maintaining a final volume of the solutions to 1 mL. The surface was stabilized with more than optimized concentration of lipoic acid-PEI and the surface stabilization was monitored by checking the aggregates formation. Finally, excess PEI-functionalized lipoic acid which would not bound to Au nanoparticle surface was eliminated by membrane centrifugation (MWCO = 30 kDa, 4000 rpm, 10 min, 3 times) and washing with pure water. The final product was collected and stored at 4 °C.

Transmittance electron microscopy (TEM) images of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complexes were obtained by JEM-1011 (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 40–100 kV. High resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis of CD particles were obtained by JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a Vertex 70 spectrophotometer (Bruker Optics, Germany). The composition of elements of CD was verified by elemental analysis using VarioELⅢ (AnalysensystemeGmbH, Germany). The bonding interactions of CD were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using ESCALAB 220i (VG Scientific, USA). The hydrodynamic volumes and surface charges of materials were measured by a Zetasizer Nano (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK). The fluorescence spectra were obtained from a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, SHIMADZU). The absorbance spectra were recorded using UV-Vis spectrophotometry (UV 2550, SHIMADZU).

Complexes of pDNA with Bare CD, CD-PEI, Au-cit, Au-PEI, and CD-PEI/Au-PEI were prepared by adding the sample suspension (5 μL) to the pDNA solution (5 μL) at various N/P ratios in pure water, and incubated for 30 min at room temperature. The complexes were then electrophoresed through a 1% (w/v) agarose gel containing ethidium bromide (EtBr, 0.5 μg/mL) in 0.5 TAE (Tris-acetate-EDTA) buffer at 100 V for 20 min. The gel was then analyzed on a UV illuminator (wiseUV® WUV, DAIHAN Scientific, Seoul, Korea).

HeLa cells (human cervical cancer cells) and PC-3 cells (human prostate cancer cells) were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Hyclone) and Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium (RPMI-1640, Hyclone), respectively with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Hyclone), 100 U/ML penicillin, and 100 μg/mL streptomycin at 37 °C in 5% CO2 humidified incubator.

In vitro transfection of CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complexes were assayed in HeLa cells and PC-3 cells. HeLa and PC-3 cells were seeded on 24-well culture plates at an initial density of 5 × 104 cells/well and incubated for 24 h in the same condition for cell culture. The complex is formed by simply mixing CD (5 μL), Au (5 μL) with 1 μg of pDNA (10 μL) at various N/P ratios. Then the cells were incubated with the complexes (20 μL) in 200 μL of serum-free media for 4 h. After 4 h, the serum-free media is washed twice using Dulbecco's phosphate buffered saline (DPBS) and further incubated in 500 μL of 10% serum-containing media for 20 h. After washing with DPBS, 200 μL of lysis buffer was added to the each well. The expression of luciferase reporter gene was monitored by a microplate spectrofluorometer (Victor3 V Multilabel Counter, Perkin–Elmer, Wellesley, MA).

In vitro cytotoxicity of CD-Au-pDNA complexes was also evaluated using MTT in HeLa and PC-3 cells. HeLa and PC-3 cells were seeded on 96-well culture plate at an initial density of 1 × 104 cells/well and incubated for 24 h in the same condition for cell culture. Complex was prepared by mixing carrier samples with 0.1 μg of pDNA and added to cells in 100 μL of serum-free media for 4 h, and further incubated for 20 h with 200 μL of 10% serum-containing media. After incubation, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was treated and incubated for 4 h. The medium was then removed, and 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to make formazan crystal salt solution. For each well, 100 μL of aliquot was transferred to new 96-well plate, and the absorption of 570 nm was measured using a microplate spectrofluorometer.

HeLa and PC-3 cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well in 12-well plate over glass coverslips. pDNA were stained with TOTO-iodide. Cells were incubated with CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complexes for 4 h, 12 h, 24 h, and 48 h at 37 °C. For a control experiment, chemically conjugated CD-PEI-Au-PEI nanoparticle forms complex with pDNA, and only CD-PEI/pDNA complex without Au-PEI were treated to cell and incubated for 4 h, 12 h, 24 h, 48 h at 37 °C. After quenching the cellular uptake by adding cold DPBS, cells were washed twice with cold DPBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min at room temperature. Cells on coverslip were mounted in Vectashield anti-fade mounting medium (Vector Labs) and observed with fluorescence microscope (Modified Zeiss Axio Observer, Z1 epi-fluorescence microscope). The magnification objective lens was 40× (Plan-Neofluar, NA = 1.30). Excitation/emission filter sets was Fura-2-380 (Ex BP 387/15, Em BP 510/90), eGFP (Ex BP 470/40, Em BP 525/50), and Cy5 (Ex BP 387/15, Em BP 770/75). The fluorescence images were analyzed with OLYMPUS FLUOVIEW ver. 1.5 Viewer software. The fluorescence signal intensity was digitalized using image J program. The total blue signal in the image was recorded and divided by cell number. The background signal was set to zero at over the entire the non-signal region. At least 3 images for each condition were selected to get the average signal intensity (n ≥ 3).

CD was synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis methods with slight modification [40]. CD was passivated in one pot procedure using PEI (MW = 25 kDa) which is one of the prevalently employed cationic polymers in gene delivery (Fig. 2 a). The synthesized CD-PEI which showed bright fluorescence emission was purified and characterized using various methods. High Angle Annular Dark Field (HAADF) Transmittance electron microscope (TEM) image demonstrated an amorphous structure of CD-PEI with 5–10 nm diameters (Fig. 2b). Also, general bright field TEM image complied with this result (Fig. S1).

CD-PEI has maximum excitation and maximum emission at 350 nm and 465 nm respectively and showed excitation-dependent emission as reported in other works (Fig. 2c) [34,35]. Moreover, by regulating excitation wavelength of CD-PEI, a broad spectrum of emission wavelength ranging from blue (∼460 nm) to green (∼540 nm), can be obtained which potentiates its application in various multi-color spectroscopy. When it was illuminated with the UV lamp, it showed bright blue color emission (Fig. 2d). Elemental analysis revealed the composition as follows; C 49.30%, H 8.88%, N 17.35%, and O 24.47%. FT-IR spectra obtained from CD-PEI and PEI showed similar spectrum pattern (Fig. 2e). However, CD-PEI showed transmittance peak at 1650 cm-1, which corresponded to vibrational stretch region of amide bond. Also, compared to CD-PEI, PEI showed stronger transmittance peak at 1570 cm−1, which corresponded to the primary or secondary amine. These FT-IR spectra indicated the successful grafting of PEI into the carbon core through chemical bonding (i.e. amide bond). Synthesized CD-PEI was further characterized using XPS analysis, and EDX analysis and it was confirmed that it had carbon and oxygen core, and its surface was functionalized with PEI while some portion of amine was grafted in the core forming amide bond (Fig. S2). The zeta potential of CD-PEI was about +30 mV; the observed highly positive at the surface predicted effective interaction with pDNA. Compared to CD-PEI, bare CD which was synthesized without PEI, showed almost neutral zeta potential, thus this positive charge comes from PEI (Fig. 2f).

Synthesis of the Au nanoparticle was carried out following the reported method with slight modification (Fig. 3 a) [41]. This particle was stabilized by citrate (Au-cit) and the TEM image of Au nanoparticle showed the size to be 16 nm in diameter. During functionalization and modification of the surface of Au nanoparticle with PEI (MW = 1.2 kDa), lipoic acid with two thiol moieties has been employed as a linker. PEI-modified lipoic acid was synthesized and the reaction was monitored by the absorbance of the N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) ester at 260 nm (Fig. 3b and Fig. S4). Though upon mixing with PEI, Au-cit underwent destabilization and concomitant aggregation as evidenced from the ensuing color changes, the PEI which was modified with lipoic acid showed good stability at various concentration of PEI-modified lipoic acid, and even at high salt concentration (Fig. 3c). And the morphology and size of Au-PEI was confirmed by TEM image (Fig. 3d). This Au-PEI showed absorbance peak at 530 nm, which was slightly different from Au-cit (520 nm) due to the surface plasmon changes, however the absorbance pattern remained similar (Fig. 3e). Also, compared to Au-cit or lipoic acid-modified Au nanoparticle (Au-lipo), the zeta potential of Au-PEI showed highly positive value which established the successful surface modification of Au nanoparticle (Fig. 3f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Association and dissociation of CD-PEI/Au-PEI assembly modulated by pDNA
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the auxiliary yet pivotal role of pDNA in facilitating the quenching, primarily effectuated by Au-PEI on the fluorescence of CD-PEI, we first tried to manipulate the surface charge of the AuNPs component. We chose Au-lipo having carboxylic moieties as negatively functionalized lipoic acid-modified Au and Au-PEI as positively functionalized components, and studied the differential quenching effects on the fluorescence of CD-PEI upon pDNA addition (Fig. 4 a). The CD-PEI was mixed separately with Au-lipo or Au-PEI, and allowed to incubate for 30 min to stabilize the interaction between CD-PEI and Au particles with different Au concentration. In case of Au-PEI, the charge repulsion between the two positively charged Au-PEI and CD-PEI prevented static quenching which necessitated direct contact; however, an increase in the concentration of Au-PEI in the bulk quite understandably increased the probability that a greater number of Au-PEI subpopulation might approach CD-PEI and fell within the Foster distance leading to dynamic quenching of CD-PEI fluorescence. On the other hand, negatively charged Au-lipo could interact with positively charged CD-PEI through direct charge interaction leading to almost complete quenching of CD-PEI fluorescence due to the static quenching. In compliance with the previously reported experiment, the difference between dynamic quenching and static quenching was found to be nearly 50% at the optimized condition (Fig. 4b) [42]. To visualize the Au-induced quenching of the CD-PEI fluorescence, CD-PEI and Au-lipo were mixed at varying concentration of Au-lipo, and as expected under UV illumination the mixtures showed gradual decrease in fluorescence intensities with the increase in Au-lipo concentrations (Fig. 4c).

A thorough investigation of Au-PEI or Au-lipo mediated quenching (dynamic and static) should provide a rudimentary knowledge which may enable us to tune and optimize the condition of pDNA-mediated quenching. To this end the fluorescence spectrum was measured for varied amount of pDNA incubated with a mixture of CD-PEI and Au-PEI for 30 min. The CD-PEI fluorescence intensity was decreased with the increase in the amount of pDNA at the fixed concentration of CD-PEI and Au-PEI (Fig. 5 a). Therefore it can be construed that the fluorescence of CD-PEI was initially quenched by dynamic quenching of Au-PEI and it eventually turned into static quenching as both CD-PEI and Au-PEI underwent complete complexation with pDNA. It could be conjectured that pDNA acted like an electrostatic glue to keep CD-PEI and Au-PEI in contact with each other. The relative fluorescence of CD-PEI at the maximum emission wavelength (465 nm) decreased linearly upon increase in amount of pDNA, and eventually became saturated. At this ratio, the TEM image of complex consisted CD-PEI, Au-PEI, and pDNA showed a size of 200 nm (Fig. 5b). The amount of PEI was then quantified, and the N/P ratio was obtained to employ this complex for the biological system as a gene delivery carrier, (Fig. S6). At the optimized CD-PEI and Au-PEI ratio which yielded effective transition from dynamic to static quenching, the N/P ratio of the selected gene delivery carrier was found to be 20 as per the PEI quantification.

As the formation of CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA molecular assembly could be accounted for the decreased fluorescence through static quenching, we envisioned that the reverse process i.e., dissociation of the complex should restore the quenched fluorescence of CD-PEI at least to some extent due to the charge repulsion-mediated parting of quencher and fluorescent particles. The anticipated transition from static to dynamic quenching could be the key to detect the dissociation of pDNA. To verify the assumption, the charge interaction ought to be reduced to achieve the recovery of fluorescence of CD-PEI and accordingly we used high concentration of salt (NaCl) which was known to destabilize the charge interaction in the model system. Generally, the competitors like heparin are commonly used to trigger the escape of pDNA from the complex [43–45], however, the quenching ability of heparin renders it inappropriate for this system as the pDNA-mediated quenching can only be transformed into heparin-mediated quenching. In essence it may lead to no fluorescence recovery. Some intracellular polycationic and polyanionic molecules are expected to trigger the dissociation of DNA from the ternary complex without interfering the recovered fluorescence intensity [43–45]. However in a simpler approach we employed a high concentration of salt to ensure total disruption of ternary complex and to validate the concept of fluorescence recovery upon dissociation before implementing the strategy into actual transfection studies. Prior to the addition of the salt, the fluorescence of CD-PEI became highly quenched by Au-PEI through static quenching, whereas incubation of the molecular complexes at high salt concentration resulted in the recovery of the fluorescence intensity of CD-PEI tantamount to the intensity observed during CD-PEI and Au-PEI dynamic quenching. Addition of varied concentration of salt in the complex led to concentration dependent displacement of the fluorescence intensity. The higher the salt concentration, the higher was the measured fluorescence intensity. The maximum emission fluorescence at 465 nm showed sigmoidal increasing pattern with the increase in the salt concentration (Fig. 5c). To visualize the fluorescence displacement, the CD-PEI/Au-PEI and CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complex, prior and after the salt treatment, were illuminated under UV light (Fig. 5d). The fluorescence of CD-PEI was almost completely quenched in the CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complex, however, interestingly the initial fluorescence intensity of CD-PEI/Au-PEI mixture was recovered almost completely after salt treatment confirming the dissociation of complex. This observation implied that the regulation of the charge interaction between CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA constituted the defining factor in monitoring the release of pDNA efficiently.

To further prove the role of complexation and subsequent dissociation in effectuating the quenching and recovery of fluorescence and also ascertain the effect of salt in inducing dissociation of the complex, CD-PEI-Au-PEI were conjugated covalently using amine-specific homofunctional linker disuccinimidyl suberate (DSS), and a comparative fluorescence study was carried out with two samples namely, the CD-PEI/Au-PEI and CD-PEI-Au-PEI conjugate, where both the carriers were complexed with pDNA and subsequently treated with NaCl salt (Fig. 6 a and b). It could be argued that any kind of permanent interaction such as chemical conjugation might nullify the fluorescence recovery by preventing CD-PEI-Au-PEI dissociation. As expected, for chemically conjugated CD-PEI-Au-PEI, the fluorescence of CD-PEI was highly quenched due to the decreased distance between CD and Au nanoparticles. Addition of pDNA to this CD-PEI-Au-PEI conjugated system induced an insignificant decrease in fluorescence intensity. Conforming to this subtle decrease in fluorescence subsequent addition of salt to the conjugate/pDNA complex induced a paltry recovery of fluorescence intensity (Fluorescence recovery ratio = 17%). The difference in fluorescence intensity between CD-PEI-Au-PEI conjugate and the corresponding pDNA complex was too small to detect at the cellular level which thwarted the utility of the CD-PEI-Au-PEI conjugated system to study pDNA and carrier dissociation. In contrast to this chemically conjugated CD-PEI-Au-PEI/pDNA, charge interaction mediated complex, i.e. CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA assembly showed dramatic changes in their fluorescence quenching and recovery (Fluorescence recovery ratio = 97%). Therefore, the simple pDNA-mediated molecular assembly system has been translated into sophisticated quenching and displacement system with a high fluorescence recovery ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gene transfection efficiency and cell viability mediated by carriers
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential of CD-PEI and Au-PEI as an efficient gene delivery carrier by confirming the formation of compact complex which is a prerequisite for efficient cellular uptake, agarose gel retardation study was conducted with various N/P ratios (Fig. 7 ). As expected, bare CD and Au-cit could not retard the pDNA band since there was no complexation between carrier and pDNA. On the other hand, PEI-modified carrier showed good complexation ability with pDNA due to their favorable charge interactions with pDNA.

The luciferase gene transfection assay was then conducted with HeLa cells and PC-3 cells (Fig. 8 a and b). The transfection efficiency of PEI25k, bare CD, Au-cit, CD-PEI, Au-PEI, and CD-PEI/Au-PEI was evaluated under optimized N/P ratios. Bare CD and Au-cit showed negligible transfection efficiency commensurate to their lack of complexation ability with pDNA. Compared to PEI25k, which is known as a gold standard for polymeric gene delivery carrier, CD-PEI and Au-PEI showed one or more than one order of reduced gene transfection efficiency at N/P ratio of 20. However, CD-PEI/Au-PEI and pDNA mixture which was endowed with excellent quenching and displacement ability through complexation and dissociation demonstrated high transfection efficiency comparable to PEI25k. Though the actual reason for this high transfection efficiency of CD-PEI/Au-PEI carrier cannot be ascertained at this point of time, it could be assumed that the appropriate size of the complex might facilitate the cellular uptake. On the other hand Au-PEI formed big aggregates (∼500 nm) compared to CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA molecular assembly (∼200 nm) at the same N/P ratio and, the large size of the complex rendered it less effective in cellular uptake (SI Fig. 10). Interestingly, in addition to the size, the hardness of the complex can also affect the cellular uptake [46–48]. Thus Au-PEI, being able to form harder complexes compared to the corresponding CD-PEI complexes, in practice might affect the intracellular uptake of pDNA into the cell adversely.

The cytotoxicity of PEI25k, bare CD, Au-cit, CD-PEI, Au-PEI, and CD-PEI/Au-PEI was evaluated using MTT assay in HeLa cells and PC-3 cells (Fig. 8c and d). Bare CD and Au-cit showed almost negligible cytotoxicity which validated the favorable biocompatibility of the core materials. In case of CD-PEI, Au-PEI and CD-PEI/Au-PEI, the cell viabilities were maintained over 80% at N/P ratio of 20. Though PEI25k is regarded as the gold standard for polymeric gene carrier, its severe cytotoxicity renders it inappropriate for future gene delivery system for in vivo biological application. The lower cytotoxicity of CD-PEI compared to PEI25k could be ascribed to the process involved in the generation of CD-PEI. Some amount of amine groups of PEI molecules were thought to be embedded within the core-shell structure of the nanoparticles during the one pot synthesis of CD-PEI and the cationic charge has been mitigated imparting high cell viability. EDX analysis confirmed the existence of N portion within the core structure (Supporting information) and complied with the previous report [40]. Au-PEI showed low cytotoxicity because the surface of Au is modified with low molecular weight PEI (PEI1.2k). Finally, the high cell viability of CD-PEI/Au-PEI carrier stemmed from the low cytotoxicity of both the components, i.e., CD-PEI and Au-PEI. To sum up, CD-PEI/Au-PEI carrier bears immense potential as gene delivery system with high efficiency and low cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular trafficking of association/dissociation of molecular assembly
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching and dissociation of CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA molecular assembly rely on the extent of complex formation and complex dissociation in the extracellular artificial environment. To demonstrate its potential applicability in the intracellular trafficking exploiting the dissociation of complex, cellular imaging was conducted by treating CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA with HeLa and PC-3 cells and monitoring fluorescence at different time interval (Fig. 9 and Fig. S12). The samples were treated in serum free condition and incubated for 4 h to allow efficient uptake of the complexes. After 4 h, the cells were further incubated with serum-containing medium at various time interval. To confirm the pDNA-mediated fluorescence quenching and displacement in the intracellular level, the pDNA was labeled with TOTO-iodide (red). CD is known to display multicolor emission depending on the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence of CD-PEI can be visualized as blue color or green color (Fig. S9–11). Just after cellular uptake (4 h), the fluorescence of CD-PEI was almost quenched. The TOTO-iodide labeled pDNA showed red emission in the cytoplasm, and the fluorescence of blue and red signal were almost merged implying co-localization of pDNA with CD-PEI and Au-PEI, and thus the complex formation between pDNA, CD-PEI and Au-PEI was clearly evident. Reduction in the fluorescence of CD-PEI further substantiated the existence of Au-PEI-induced static quenching where pDNA strung them together through electrostatic interactions. The fluorescence after 4 h cellular uptake followed by further incubation for 8 h (12 h overall) showed a slight increase in the fluorescence intensity of CD-PEI, and pDNA remained mostly co-localized with CD-PEI. With the increase in incubation period, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually and the fluorescence signal were spread across the cytoplasm (Fig. 9a). Also, the pDNA moved into the nucleus for its transcription after cellular uptake (Fig. 9b). As the carrier and pDNA resided at different locations inside the cell after 24 h and 48 h of treatment with the complex, the fluorescence intensity of CD-PEI grew stronger and the fluorescence signals dispersed all over the cytoplasm.

To examine the fluorescence quenching and displacement arising from the charge interaction between pDNA and CD-PEI/Au-PEI, chemically conjugated CD-PEI-Au-PEI was allowed to form complex with pDNA and subjected to cell transfection and imaging. Chemical conjugation between CD-PEI and Au-PEI confined them within the Foster distance overpowering the charge repulsion-mediated segregation of the two particles, CD-PEI, a fluorophore and Au-PEI, the quencher. Thus the CD-PEI and Au-PEI remained chemically tied-up eliciting permanent static quenching of CD-PEI (Fig. 6b). The intracellular imaging also complied with this intuitive assumption. The fluorescence of CD-PEI was quenched almost completely just after cellular uptake (4 h) demonstrating similar observation as experienced for the simple charge interaction generated complex made of CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA. However, the two concerned complexes differed with respect to the acquired quenching at the onset of cellular uptake. Unlike the CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA assembly (Fig. 9a) obtained from physical mixing, the complex of chemically conjugated CD-PEI-Au-PEI showed weak fluorescence intensity even after long incubation time (Fig. 10 a, upper) and it can be interpreted as the result of steric-nonconformity imposed by the linker preventing complete quenching through maintaining enough distance between them to exhibit fluorescence even after the dissociation of pDNA intracellularly. Moreover, the role of Au-PEI in mediating fluorescence quenching and displacement of CD-PEI has been ascertained by conducting the cellular imaging without Au-PEI. As expected the fluorescence intensity of CD-PEI remained very strong in the absence of Au-PEI even at the onset of cellular uptake (4 h) and after long incubation time. CD-PEI were mostly localized in an aggregated manner inside the cell initially and eventually the fluorescence of CD-PEI became well spread intracellularly at extended time interval after cellular uptake (Fig. 10a, down).

To analyze quantitatively the cellular uptake and dissociation of pDNA from nanoassembly, blue signal from CD was digitalized (Fig. 10b). The gradual increase in the intensity of the blue signal from CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA molecular assembly implied apparent dissociation of pDNA from carriers during intracellular process. The blue signal intensity at an incubation time of 48 h was found to be 5-fold stronger than that observed after 4 h of incubation. In contrast, chemically conjugated CD-PEI-Au-PEI system showed only insignificant or no enhancement in signal intensity and the signal remained weak throughout the incubation time. In case of CD-PEI/pDNA system without Au-PEI, strong blue signal intensity was observed throughout incubation time and the strong fluorescence signal in the absence of any quencher during intracellular process justified the employment of the fluorophore-quencher combination.

The CD-PEI changed its fluorescence signal only in the presence of Au-PEI, and the charge repulsion after the dissociation of pDNA from CD-PEI and Au-PEI under intracellular condition would make the fluorescence displaced. Therefore, the CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA molecular assembly worked well intracellularly by complexation and dissociation with pDNA. Thus, we could monitor the dissociation of molecular assembly using this carrier system through simple monitoring of the fluorescence intensity intracellularly without employing any complicated modification of pDNA. In essence, the charge interaction between CD-PEI/Au-PEI/pDNA complex governed the dissociation-association of the system and the fluorescence property of CD was exploited ingeniously to track the dissociation-association process.

pDNA-facilitated complexation with two carrier systems namely fluorescent nanoparticle and the quencher nanoparticles not only plays a crucial role in imparting excellent physicochemical properties to the polyplex deemed ideal for efficient cellular uptake but also confers unique optical properties which could provide a highly intriguing method to monitor the complexation and especially the dissociation of polyplex inside the cytoplasm after cellular uptake. The work demonstrated the pDNA orchestrated quenching of fluorescence during polyplex formation with subsequent release of pDNA resulting in recovery of fluorescence. Unlike the other reported system [20,25–27], the strategy requires label free pDNA. In addition to the high gene transfection efficiency and low cytotoxicity the developed system provides the provision to track the payloads intracellularly through simple fluorescence study. Judicious utilization of the strategy could broaden the scope and practical implication significantly not only in improving gene delivery but also in gaining insight into the mechanistic aspect of gene delivery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Research Center Program of IBS (Institute for Basic Science) in Korea (CA1203-02). We thank Prof. J. Doh for assistance in the fluorescence microscopy analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2013.05.072.
